content:
# Genesis Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3r–3v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_004_page_001.jpg, GEN_004_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** And the man knew Eve his wife, and she conceived and bore Cain, and she said, "I have gotten a man with the LORD."

**2** And she continued to bear his brother Abel. Now Abel was a keeper of flocks, and Cain was a tiller of the ground.

**3** And in the course of time it came to pass that Cain brought an offering to the LORD from the fruit of the ground.

**4** And Abel — he also brought from the firstborn of his flock and from their fat portions. And the LORD looked with favor upon Abel and his offering,

**5** but upon Cain and his offering He did not look with favor. And Cain was very angry, and his face fell.

**6** And the LORD said to Cain, "Why are you angry, and why has your face fallen?

**7** Is it not so that if you do well, there is a lifting up? But if you do not do well, sin is crouching at the door. And toward you is its desire, but you — you must rule over it."

**8** And Cain spoke to Abel his brother. And it came to pass when they were in the field that Cain rose up against Abel his brother and killed him.

**9** And the LORD said to Cain, "Where is Abel your brother?" And he said, "I do not know. Am I my brother's keeper?"

**10** And He said, "What have you done? The voice of your brother's blood is crying out to Me from the ground.

**11** And now, cursed are you from the ground, which has opened its mouth to receive your brother's blood from your hand.

**12** When you work the ground, it shall no longer yield its strength to you. A wanderer and a fugitive you shall be on the earth."

**13** And Cain said to the LORD, "My iniquity is too great to bear.

**14** Behold, You have driven me out this day from the face of the ground, and from Your face I shall be hidden. And I shall be a wanderer and a fugitive on the earth, and it shall be that whoever finds me will kill me."

**15** And the LORD said to him, "Therefore, whoever kills Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold." And the LORD set a mark on Cain, lest anyone finding him strike him down.

**16** And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD and dwelt in the land of Nod, east of Eden.

**17** And Cain knew his wife, and she conceived and bore Enoch. And he was building a city and called the name of the city after the name of his son, Enoch.

**18** And to Enoch was born Irad, and Irad fathered Mehujael, and Mehujael fathered Methushael, and Methushael fathered Lamech.

**19** And Lamech took for himself two wives. The name of the one was Adah, and the name of the second was Zillah.

**20** And Adah bore Jabal. He was the father of those who dwell in tents and keep livestock.

**21** And the name of his brother was Jubal. He was the father of all those who play the harp and the pipe.

**22** And Zillah — she also bore Tubal-cain, a forger of every cutting instrument of bronze and iron. And the sister of Tubal-cain was Naamah.

**23** And Lamech said to his wives:

"Adah and Zillah, hear my voice;
wives of Lamech, give ear to my speech.
For a man I have killed for my wound,
and a young man for my bruise.

**24** If Cain is avenged sevenfold,
then Lamech seventy-sevenfold."

**25** And Adam knew his wife again, and she bore a son and called his name Seth, for she said, "God has appointed for me another offspring in place of Abel, for Cain killed him."

**26** And to Seth — to him also a son was born, and he called his name Enosh. At that time it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of three manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 4 spans Folios 3r–3v. The characteristic three-column Tiberian format continues uninterrupted from Genesis 3. Both folios were read directly. Folio 3r (page 001) contains the bulk of the chapter through approximately verse 22, with folio 3v (page 002) carrying the chapter through verse 26 and into Genesis 5. The divine name יהוה appears in its full Tiberian vocalization throughout, and the parashah divisions are marked in the margins. Notably, the divine name transitions in this chapter from יהוה אֱלֹהִים (LORD God, used throughout Gen 2–3) to יהוה alone — a shift that begins at 4:1 and continues for the remainder of chapter 4. The Masorah parva notations in the margins of the Leningrad Codex flag several text-critical points noted below.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The Genesis 4 page was read directly from the manuscript image. The page header reads Γ. ΚΑΠ. ΙΙΙΙ (CAP. IIII) in the margin, identifying the chapter clearly. The Greek text is written in the characteristic three-column uncial hand without word spaces. Chapter 4 in Vaticanus contains several significant variants from the MT, documented below.

**Additional reference:** Dead Sea Scrolls 4QGen-b and fragments from 4QGen-j (consulted for this chapter where they preserve text).

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת יְהוָה (qaniti ish et-YHWH) — "I have gotten a man with the LORD"** (v. 1)

This is one of the most debated lines in Genesis. The Hebrew reads: וַתֹּאמֶר קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת יְהוָה — "and she said, I have gotten / acquired a man, et-YHWH." The crux is the function of אֶת (et) before יהוה. In the simplest reading, אֶת here is the preposition meaning "with, together with" — and the translation is "I have gotten a man with the LORD" or "with the help of the LORD." This is the majority traditional rendering (KJV, ESV, NASB, JPS).

A minority reading, ancient and persistent, takes the אֶת as the direct object marker (the untranslatable particle that normally marks a definite direct object) and renders: "I have gotten a man — YHWH" or "I have gotten a man, even YHWH." On this reading, Eve is making a theological identification: the man she has borne is, in some sense, YHWH. This reading was known in antiquity (Onkelos renders it paraphrastically; certain rabbinic sources discuss it), and has been championed in modern scholarship as the original reading. It would represent one of the earliest messianic readings embedded in the text itself: the promised seed (Gen 3:15) understood as divine.

The translation adopts the prepositional reading — "with the LORD" — as it is linguistically the simpler construction and is the reading of the LXX (ἐκτησάμην ἄνθρωπον διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ, "I have gotten a man through God"). The Vaticanus text here reads διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("through God"), which substitutes θεοῦ for יהוה and uses διά ("through, by means of") as a transparent prepositional rendering. This confirms that the LXX translator read אֶת as the preposition. The minority direct-object reading is documented here as a live exegetical tradition that the Hebrew grammar does not rule out.

**2. שְׁאֵת (se'et) and "lifting up" in verse 7** (v. 7)

Verse 7 is one of the most syntactically broken verses in the entire Hebrew Bible, and no translation resolves it without interpretation. The Hebrew of the MT reads:

הֲלוֹא אִם תֵּיטִיב שְׂאֵת וְאִם לֹא תֵיטִיב לַפֶּתַח חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל בּוֹ

Literally: "Is it not so — if you do well, lifting up? And if you do not do well, at the door sin crouches. And toward you is its desire, but you shall rule over it."

The difficulties are multiple:

(a) שְׂאֵת (se'et) — the noun from the root נשא, "to lift, to carry." It means "a lifting up, an exaltation, a carrying." But: lifting up of what? The face (פָּנִים, panim) is implied by context — in v. 6, God asks "why has your face fallen?" and the implied counterpart is "if you do well, your face will be lifted up" (i.e., accepted, favored). Some translations make this explicit: "you will be accepted" (NIV), "will you not be lifted up?" (ESV). The formal equivalence approach renders the bare noun phrase — "there is a lifting up" — preserving the ellipsis of the Hebrew rather than filling it in.

(b) לַפֶּתַח חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ (lapetach chatta't rovets) — "at the door sin crouches / is crouching." The verb רָבַץ (ravats) is used of animals lying down or crouching in wait, particularly of a lion or a predator at rest before a spring. The image is vivid: sin as a crouching beast at the threshold. The construction is stark: there is no particle connecting this clause to the preceding condition; the syntax breaks. "If you do not do well" is followed immediately by "sin crouching at the door" — the implied copula ("there is sin crouching") must be supplied by the reader. This grammatical rupture is retained in the translation by not adding a smooth connective. The formal rendering allows the broken syntax to stand as it stands in the Hebrew.

(c) וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ (ve-elekha teshugato) — "and toward you is its desire." The same word תְּשׁוּקָה (teshuqah) used in 3:16 for the woman's desire toward her husband now recurs, applied to sin's desire toward Cain. This is one of only three occurrences of this rare word in the entire Hebrew Bible (3:16; 4:7; Song 7:11). The deliberate echo is unmistakable: as in 3:16, the relationship involves a desire that is directional and potentially overwhelming — and a corresponding summons to rule (תִּמְשָׁל, timshal). The structural parallel between 3:16 and 4:7 (desire + rule) is preserved in the translation and noted explicitly here. The LXX renders 4:7's teshuqah with ἡ ἀποστροφὴ αὐτοῦ ("its turning"), the same word (ἀποστροφή) the LXX used for teshuqah in 3:16.

The translation renders verse 7 as closely to the MT as English permits without syntactic reconstruction, preserving the rupture and the starkness of the Hebrew.

**3. עֲוֹנִי גָּדוֹל מִנְּשֹׂא (avoni gadol min-so) — "My iniquity is too great to bear"** (v. 13)

This verse carries one of the densest lexical ambiguities in Genesis 4. The word עָוֹן ('avon) denotes both the act of iniquity (the sin itself) and its consequence (the punishment, the guilt-bearing). The same ambiguity attends the phrase since the root appears in both senses throughout the Hebrew Bible (cf. Lev 16:22 for punishment-bearing; Ezek 18:20 for guilt). The double meaning cannot be resolved by the Hebrew alone; the context activates both.

Furthermore, the verb מִנְּשֹׂא (min-so', from נשא) means "to carry, to bear, to lift, to forgive." In a single stroke: "My iniquity/punishment is too great to be borne/forgiven." Can it mean "My punishment is too great to bear"? Yes. Can it mean "My iniquity is too great to be forgiven"? Yes. Both are live readings of exactly the same words, and Cain may intend both simultaneously or may not clearly distinguish them — the text refuses to clarify.

Traditional translations split the ambiguity: KJV "My punishment is greater than I can bear," ESV "My punishment is greater than I can bear," NASB "My punishment is too great to bear," but LXX (Vaticanus) reads μείζων ἡ αἰτία μου τοῦ ἀφεθῆναί με — "My guilt/charge is too great to be forgiven/released." The LXX takes the "forgiveness" reading of נשא and the "guilt/charge" reading of עָוֹן. This is a significant divergence: the MT's Cain may be lamenting a punishment too heavy; the LXX's Cain is despairing of forgiveness entirely. Neither reading fully excludes the other from the Hebrew, and the double meaning is theologically rich: the iniquity is so great it cannot be carried — whether by Cain in bearing the punishment, or by God in bearing away the sin.

The translation renders "My iniquity is too great to bear" — using "iniquity" rather than "punishment" to preserve the moral dimension of 'avon, and "to bear" rather than "to be forgiven" to preserve the literal value of נשא. The translation notes make both readings explicit. This is a point at which formal equivalence necessarily cannot convey the full semantic weight of the Hebrew without annotation.

**4. אוֹת (ot) — "a mark"** (v. 15)

וַיָּשֶׂם יְהוָה לְקַיִן אוֹת (vayyasem YHWH le-Qayin ot) — "And the LORD set for Cain a sign/mark." The word אוֹת (ot) is the standard Hebrew word for "sign" — the same word used for the signs in the heavens (Gen 1:14), the sign of the rainbow (Gen 9:12, 13, 17), the sign of circumcision (Gen 17:11), and the signs Moses performs before Pharaoh (Exod 4:8ff). It is never used elsewhere in the sense of a stigma or brand. The "mark of Cain" in popular tradition is an identifying scar or brand of shame; the Hebrew simply says God set a sign for him. The nature of the sign is not described. "Mark" in English carries the stigma-connotation of "marked man," which is partly right — the sign protects Cain from retribution — but the Hebrew ot is neither inherently a mark of shame nor a brand. The translation uses "mark" as the conventional English rendering while noting that "sign" is equally accurate and perhaps more theologically loaded.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders it σημεῖον τῷ Καϊν (semeion to Kain), using σημεῖον ("sign, omen, mark"), which is the standard Greek translation of אוֹת throughout the LXX. This confirms that the ancient Greek tradition heard "sign" rather than "brand."

**5. נוֹד (Nod) — "wandering"** (v. 16)

The land of Nod (אֶרֶץ נוֹד, erets Nod) takes its name from the Hebrew root נוד (nud), "to wander, to flee, to be a vagabond" — the same root used in the curse at v. 12 (נָע וָנָד, na va-nad, "a wanderer and a fugitive"). Cain is exiled to the land that bears the name of his condition. The English translation retains "Nod" as a proper name (it is a place name) but the wordplay — wanderer in the land of wandering — is visible to any Hebrew reader. It is noted here rather than inserted into the text.

**6. הוּחַל לִקְרֹא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה (huchal liquro beshem YHWH) — "it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD"** (v. 26)

The final verse of Genesis 4 is another crux. The Hebrew Hof'al (passive) of חלל — הוּחַל (huchal) — means "it was begun." The LXX (Vaticanus) renders οὗτος ἤλπισεν ἐπικαλεῖσθαι τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ — "this one hoped to call upon the name of the LORD God" — replacing the impersonal passive with a personal subject (Enosh) and "hoped" (ἤλπισεν) rather than "began." This is a substantial LXX variant: the MT records the historical beginning of calling on the name, while the LXX says Enosh hoped to do so — a more cautious or eschatological framing.

The MT rendering "it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD" is followed. The passive construction is retained as in the Hebrew, without inserting a subject ("people began" or "men began," as in many translations). The impersonal passive leaves the subject open: who began? The grammar does not say. This is the last verse before the toledot of Adam (Gen 5:1), and its placement as the close of the Eden-to-Exile narrative (2:4–4:26) gives it structural weight: the chapter that records the first murder closes with the first prayer.

**7. Lamech's Song (vv. 23–24) — Poetic Structure**

Verses 23–24 contain the oldest recoverable poem in Genesis, sometimes called the Sword Song or the Song of Lamech. It is written in clear 3:3 or 3:2 Hebrew meter with synthetic parallelism:

Line 1: שִׁמְעַן קוֹלִי / נְשֵׁי לֶמֶךְ — "Hear my voice / wives of Lamech" (vocative + vocative)
Line 2: הַאֲזֵנָּה אִמְרָתִי — "give ear to my speech" (imperative + object)
Line 3: כִּי אִישׁ הָרַגְתִּי לְפִצְעִי — "For a man I have killed for my wound"
Line 4: וְיֶלֶד לְחַבֻּרָתִי — "and a young man for my bruise"
Line 5: כִּי שִׁבְעָתַיִם יֻקַּם קָיִן — "For Cain is avenged sevenfold"
Line 6: וְלֶמֶךְ שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה — "and Lamech seventy-sevenfold"

The song is an act of violent boasting — Lamech declares that he has killed a man for merely wounding him, and that he needs no divine protection because his retribution vastly exceeds even Cain's. The escalation from Cain's sevenfold protection (v. 15) to Lamech's seventy-sevenfold vengeance is the theological climax of the chapter's descent: the chapter that begins with birth and an offering to the LORD closes with a song of unlimited retributive violence. The poetic structure is preserved in the translation by arranging the lines as verse. "Seventy-sevenfold" (שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה) is literally "seventy and seven," which can mean "seventy-seven times" or "seventy-seven-fold." The LXX reads ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά, "seventy times seven" (490), which differs from the MT's "seventy-and-seven" (77). The MT is followed; the LXX variant is noted.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 4

The Vaticanus manuscript page for Genesis 4 was read directly. The following are the principal variant readings and their bearing on this translation.

**4:1 — διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("through God") vs. אֶת יְהוָה**

As noted above, the LXX renders אֶת יְהוָה with διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("through God"), substituting the generic θεός for the divine name יהוה and using the preposition διά ("through, by means of") for the Hebrew אֶת. This confirms that the LXX translator read אֶת as the preposition "with/through" and not as the direct object marker. The translation follows this reading while preserving the divine name יהוה as "the LORD" in accordance with established convention.

**4:7 — LXX substantial expansion**

The LXX (Vaticanus) text of verse 7 is substantially different from the MT and reads as an interpretive paraphrase:

οὐκ ἐὰν ὀρθῶς προσενέγκῃς, ὀρθῶς δὲ μὴ διέλῃς, ἥμαρτες; ἡσύχασον· πρὸς σὲ ἡ ἀποστροφὴ αὐτοῦ, καὶ σὺ ἄρξεις αὐτοῦ.

"Is it not so that if you offer rightly, but do not divide rightly, you have sinned? Be still. Its turning is toward you, and you shall rule over it."

This is a remarkable divergence. The LXX smooths out the broken Hebrew syntax of the MT entirely: it restructures the conditions into a complete sentence ("if you offer rightly but do not divide rightly, you have sinned"), adds the command "Be still" (ἡσύχασον), and omits the image of sin crouching at the door entirely. The sin as a crouching beast — the most vivid image in the Hebrew verse — is simply absent from the LXX. "Its turning" (ἡ ἀποστροφὴ αὐτοῦ) for תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ continues the LXX's pattern of rendering תְּשׁוּקָה as ἀποστροφή (as in 3:16). The MT is followed throughout v. 7; the LXX's restructuring is noted as evidence of the ancient interpretive difficulty of this verse.

**4:8 — LXX adds "Let us go into the field"**

The MT of v. 8 reads: וַיֹּאמֶר קַיִן אֶל הֶבֶל אָחִיו — "And Cain spoke to Abel his brother" — without recording what Cain said. The LXX supplies what is missing: Καὶ εἶπεν Κάιν πρὸς Ἅβελ τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ· διέλθωμεν εἰς τὸ πεδίον — "And Cain said to Abel his brother, 'Let us go out into the field.'" The Samaritan Pentateuch similarly fills in the speech. The MT's silence is the lectio difficilior: "Cain spoke" — and what he said is unknown. The MT's ellipsis is retained in the translation; the LXX/Samaritan addition is noted. Many modern translations supply something (NASB: "Cain said to Abel his brother. And it came about when they were in the field"); the translation follows the MT and allows "Cain spoke to Abel his brother" to stand without filling in the content.

**4:13 — μείζων ἡ αἰτία μου τοῦ ἀφεθῆναί με ("My guilt is too great to be forgiven")**

As noted above (translation decision 3), the LXX takes 'avon as "guilt/charge" (αἰτία, literally "cause, charge, accusation") and naso' as "to forgive/release" (ἀφεθῆναι, aorist passive infinitive of ἀφίημι, "to forgive, to release"). This produces "My guilt/charge is too great to be released/forgiven" — a despair of forgiveness reading rather than a complaint about punishment. The MT's double meaning of both "punishment too heavy" and "iniquity too great to forgive" underlies both traditions. "My iniquity is too great to bear" is retained from the MT.

**4:24 — ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά ("seventy times seven") vs. שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה ("seventy-and-seven")**

As noted above (translation decision 7), the LXX reads "seventy times seven" (490) against the MT's "seventy-and-seven" (77). This variant is significant for the New Testament: Jesus, in Matt 18:22, uses ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά when instructing Peter on forgiveness — almost certainly in deliberate inversion of the LXX's Lamech. Whether Jesus is citing the LXX version of Lamech's song (490) or the MT version (77) inverted, the interpretive counter-movement is the same: where Lamech boasts of seventy-sevenfold vengeance, Jesus calls for seventy-sevenfold (or seventy-times-seven-fold) forgiveness. The MT is followed; the LXX variant and its New Testament echo are noted.

---

### Summary Table — Genesis 4 Manuscript Comparison

| Verse | MT | LXX (Vaticanus) | DSS (4QGen-b/j) | Translation | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4:1 | אֶת יְהוָה (with/[object] LORD) | διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("through God") | Fragmentary; MT probable | "with the LORD" | LXX prepositional; direct-object alternative noted |
| 4:7 | sin crouching at the door | No crouching image; "be still" added | Fragmentary | MT preserved verbatim | LXX smooths broken syntax; crouching image absent |
| 4:8 | Cain "spoke" — content omitted | "Let us go into the field" added | Sam. Pent. also adds speech | MT ellipsis preserved | LXX/Sam. fill the gap; MT silence retained |
| 4:13 | עָוֹן — iniquity/punishment (ambiguous) | αἰτία — guilt/charge | Confirms MT | "My iniquity is too great to bear" | LXX reads "too great to be forgiven"; double meaning noted |
| 4:15 | אוֹת — sign/mark | σημεῖον — sign | Confirms MT | "mark" | LXX confirms sign-reading; stigma connotation is later tradition |
| 4:24 | שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה — seventy-and-seven | ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά — seventy times seven | Fragmentary | "seventy-sevenfold" | LXX 490 vs. MT 77; NT echo in Matt 18:22 noted |
| 4:26 | הוּחַל — "it was begun" (passive) | ἤλπισεν — "he hoped" | Confirms MT | "it was begun to call upon" | LXX adds personal subject + hope; MT passive retained |

**Overall assessment:** Genesis 4 contains a higher density of text-critical and translation challenges than Genesis 1–3. The most significant divergences between the MT and LXX in this chapter are (1) the expansion of v. 7, where the LXX entirely restructures the broken syntax and omits the crouching-sin image; (2) the filling of the ellipsis in v. 8, where the LXX and Samaritan Pentateuch supply Cain's invitation; (3) the ambiguity of v. 13, where MT and LXX read opposite poles of the double meaning of 'avon and naso'; and (4) the Lamech variant in v. 24. At every point the MT is followed as the primary text, with the LXX variants documented. The DSS evidence for this chapter is fragmentary and does not require departure from the Leningrad Codex tradition.

---

### Structural and Theological Note

Genesis 4 closes the toledot of heaven and earth (2:4–4:26), the narrative unit that began with the creation of the man in 2:7. The chapter is structured around two parallel genealogical-narrative movements: (A) the Cain story (vv. 1–16), which descends from birth through offering, murder, exile, and Cain's city-building; and (B) the genealogy of Cain through Lamech (vv. 17–24), which descends through the development of culture (herding, music, metalwork) and ends in the boasting violence of Lamech's song. Against both descents, a counter-movement closes the chapter: (C) the birth of Seth, the naming of Enosh, and the beginning of calling on the name of the LORD (vv. 25–26).

The divine name shifts in this chapter from the compound יהוה אֱלֹהִים (the LORD God) of Genesis 2–3 to יהוה alone. This is not an accident of sources but a textual datum that the formal translation registers faithfully: from 4:1 onward, the narrator and the characters speak of and to יהוה alone. The theological weight of this shift is not explained within the chapter; it is a feature of the text as given.

Key terminological continuities with Genesis 1–3 maintained in this translation:
- "the LORD" for יהוה (consistent throughout)
- "man" and "Adam" for אָדָם (v. 25 returns to "Adam" as the personal name of the first man)
- "ground" (אֲדָמָה, adamah) continues as the word for the soil from which Adam was taken and to which Cain is cursed in relation (vv. 10, 11, 12, 14)
- תְּשׁוּקָה ("desire") at v. 7 is rendered with exact consistency with its use at 3:16, preserving the structural echo between the woman's desire for her husband and sin's desire for Cain

The chapter that opens with birth — "I have gotten a man" — closes with prayer: "it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD." The trajectory from murder to worship is the arc of Genesis 4; whether it represents hope or merely the persistence of human civilization alongside its violence, the text leaves for the reader to carry.

— LOGOS
